17.1.title: Arjuna's question: śraddhā without śāstra
17.1.body: Arjuna's compound शास्त्रविधिम् (द्वितीया एकवचन, object of उत्सृज्य — absolutive 'having set aside') frames the dilemma: people who abandon scriptural injunction yet worship श्रद्धया अन्विताः (तृतीया, instrumental 'endowed with faith'). The question तेषां निष्ठा तु का — 'what is their standing?' — uses षष्ठी बहुवचन तेषाम् and forces Krishna to classify faith itself by guṇa.

17.1.title: सत्त्वमाहो रजस्तमः — the threefold doubt
17.1.body: The disjunction आहो ('or rather') strings together three nominatives — सत्त्वम्, रजः, तमः — leaving the निष्ठा's nature open. Arjuna's framing already anticipates the chapter's whole architecture: every religious act will be sorted by which guṇa drives the underlying faith.

17.2.title: त्रिविधा श्रद्धा — faith born of svabhāva
17.2.body: श्रद्धा is स्वभावजा (उपपद तत्पुरुष — स्वभावात् जायते इति, स्त्रीलिंग प्रथमा agreeing with श्रद्धा) — 'born of one's own nature.' The three adjectives सात्त्विकी, राजसी, तामसी are all feminine, agreeing with श्रद्धा, and the deictic तां शृणु ('listen to that') uses द्वितीया एकवचन to point Arjuna toward the exposition that follows.

17.2.title: देहिनाम् — faith belongs to the embodied
17.2.body: The षष्ठी बहुवचन देहिनाम् ('of embodied beings') localises śraddhā in the देही — not an abstract metaphysical principle but the lived orientation of a person with a body and a history. स्वभावजा thus quietly admits that the guṇa-mix one is born into pre-shapes the faith one is capable of.

17.3.title: यो यच्छ्रद्धः स एव सः — you are your faith
17.3.body: The famous formula uses three correlative pronouns (यः … यत् … सः) and a बहुव्रीहि यच्छ्रद्धः ('he whose faith is of which sort'). The predicate identity स एव सः collapses person and faith: a man is no more and no less than the object he reveres. श्रद्धामयः (मयट् suffix, 'consisting of faith') makes the point morphologically.

17.3.title: सत्त्वानुरूपा — faith mirrors inner sattva
17.3.body: सत्त्वानुरूपा ('conforming to one's सत्त्व') is a तत्पुरुष agreeing with श्रद्धा; here सत्त्व means 'inner constitution,' not the guṇa narrowly. The verse universalises (सर्वस्य, षष्ठी एकवचन — 'of everyone') what 17.2 said of देहिनः: there is no faith floating free of the faither.

17.4.title: Three objects of worship, three guṇas
17.4.body: The verb यजन्ते (लट्, आत्मनेपद, बहुवचन — 'they worship') governs three द्वितीया objects across the verse: देवान् (सात्त्विकाः), यक्षरक्षांसि (राजसाः), and प्रेतान् भूतगणान् (तामसाः). The grammatical parallel makes Krishna's claim crisp — what you worship indexes what you are.

17.4.title: अन्ये … जनाः — the impersonal tāmasa
17.4.body: The tamasic worshippers are not even named by their attitude (as the rajasic ones were); they are merely अन्ये जनाः ('other people') and तामसाः. The distancing is rhetorical: lower guṇa is described with less interiority, more sociological flatness.

17.5.title: अशास्त्रविहितं तपः — penance outside the canon
17.5.body: The object तपः (नपुंसक द्वितीया) is qualified by two long compounds: अशास्त्रविहितम् ('not enjoined by śāstra' — नञ्तत्पुरुष) and घोरम् ('terrible'). The agents are described by दम्भाहङ्कारसंयुक्ताः and कामरागबलान्विताः — both प्रथमा बहुवचन तृतीया-तत्पुरुष compounds (संयुक्ताः / अन्विताः with instrumental prior members) piling up the inner deformities that drive showy asceticism.

17.5.title: तप्यन्ते — heating oneself, literally
17.5.body: तप्यन्ते is लट् आत्मनेपद from √तप् ('to heat'), and the cognate accusative तपः keeps that heat-metaphor live: these people 'heat a heat.' The grammar itself makes the ascetic look like a man stoking his own ego while calling it discipline.

17.6.title: कर्षयन्तः — torturing the body's bhūta-grāma
17.6.body: The participle कर्षयन्तः (शतृ, प्रथमा बहुवचन of causative √कृष् — 'causing to be drawn/emaciated') governs the द्वितीया भूतग्रामम् ('the assembly of elements') located शरीरस्थम्. The shocking move is the next द्वितीया — माम् … अन्तःशरीरस्थम् — Krishna himself, dwelling inside the body, is being tortured along with the elements.

17.6.title: तान् विद्धि आसुरनिश्चयान् — know them as demonic
17.6.body: विद्धि (लोट् मध्यम एकवचन of √विद्) takes a double accusative: तान् ('them') and आसुरनिश्चयान् ('of demonic resolve' — बहुव्रीहि). Krishna does not call them ignorant or weak; he reclassifies their entire निश्चय as आसुर, the same category that the whole of chapter 16 anatomised.

17.7.title: Even food is threefold — आहारः … त्रिविधः
17.7.body: The तु in आहारस्त्वपि (= आहारः तु अपि) signals a fresh angle: not just worship and tapas, but even food (आहारः, पुल्लिंग प्रथमा) splits three ways. प्रियः ('dear/preferred,' प्रथमा agreeing with आहारः) is the key word — Krishna will classify food by what each guṇa-type finds tasty, not by some objective nutritional axis.

17.7.title: तेषां भेदम् — listen to the distinction
17.7.body: तेषाम् (षष्ठी बहुवचन) refers forward to the quartet about to be enumerated: यज्ञः, तपः, दानम्, आहारः. भेदम् इमम् शृणु makes this verse a header for the rest of the chapter — every following classification is a child of this single 'threefold' frame.

17.8.title: Sāttvika food — the six-fold vital effect
17.8.body: The opening compound आयुःसत्त्वबलारोग्यसुखप्रीतिविवर्धनाः is a single प्रथमा बहुवचन तत्पुरुष: 'increasers of lifespan, vitality, strength, health, happiness, and affection.' Four short adjectives — रस्याः (juicy), स्निग्धाः (oily/unctuous), स्थिराः (substantial/lasting), हृद्याः (heart-pleasing) — describe the food itself, while सात्त्विकप्रियाः ('dear to the sāttvika') names the taster.

17.8.title: Effect-first definition of sattva
17.8.body: Note the grammar: the long compound listing six benefits comes before the four short epithets — Krishna defines sāttvika food by what it does to a person, not by ritual purity. The plurals throughout (आहाराः, सात्त्विकप्रियाः) keep the category open, not a closed menu.

17.9.title: The rājasa six tastes — कट्वम्ल…विदाहिनः
17.9.body: The compound कट्वम्ललवणात्युष्णतीक्ष्णरूक्षविदाहिनः (प्रथमा बहुवचन of आहाराः) packs seven tastes/qualities into one word: bitter, sour, salty, very-hot, pungent, dry, burning. The genitive राजसस्य ('of the rajasic person,' षष्ठी एकवचन) locates the desire — these foods are इष्टाः ('desired') by the rajasic, not inherently bad.

17.9.title: दुःखशोकामयप्रदाः — sorrow, grief, illness
17.9.body: The trailing compound दुःखशोकामयप्रदाः ('bestowers of pain, grief, and disease' — तत्पुरुष + कर्तृवाचक प्रद) records the consequence. Same grammatical position as 17.8's six-fold benefits, but now negative: Krishna mirrors the structure deliberately so the contrast lands without commentary.

17.10.title: Tāmasa food — stale, foul, leftover
17.10.body: Four neuter singular adjectives describe the food: यातयामम् ('that whose याम — a three-hour watch — has passed,' बहुव्रीहि = stale), गतरसम् ('with taste gone' — बहुव्रीहि), पूति ('rotten'), पर्युषितम् ('kept overnight'). The यत् pronoun (नपुंसक प्रथमा एकवचन) makes 'whatever' such food is the antecedent; तामसप्रियम् (तत्पुरुष) names the eater.

17.10.title: उच्छिष्टम् and अमेध्यम् — ritually impure
17.10.body: उच्छिष्टम् ('leavings,' food already partially eaten by another) and अमेध्यम् ('unfit for offering / impure' — नञ्तत्पुरुष from मेध्य) add the ritual axis to the sensory one. The तामसा eater does not even respect the offering-fit/leftover distinction the śāstra cares about.

17.11.title: अफलाकाङ्क्षिभिः — yajña without fruit-craving
17.11.body: The instrumental अफलाकाङ्क्षिभिः (तृतीया बहुवचन, उपपद तत्पुरुष — 'by those who do not crave fruit') marks the agent. The yajña is विधिदृष्टः (तृतीया तत्पुरुष — 'seen/enjoined by rule'), and the inner stance is captured in यष्टव्यम् एव इति मनः समाधाय ('having settled the mind on: it simply must be performed'). यष्टव्यम् is the कृत्य/gerundive of √यज्.

17.11.title: मनः समाधाय — the inner posture defines sāttvika
17.11.body: समाधाय is an absolutive (ल्यप् after सम्-आ-धा): the agent first composes his mind around 'this is to be done as duty,' and the external rite follows. The whole verse defines sāttvika yajña not by what is offered but by the kāraṇa-state behind it.

17.12.title: अभिसन्धाय फलम् — rājasa yajña aims at result
17.12.body: अभिसन्धाय (absolutive of अभि-सम्-धा — 'having aimed at') with object फलम् (नपुंसक द्वितीया) is the precise opposite of 17.11's अफलाकाङ्क्षिभिः. The verb इज्यते (लट् कर्मणि — 'is offered') keeps the rite formally intact; only the motive shifts.

17.12.title: दम्भार्थम् — yajña as display
17.12.body: दम्भार्थम् ('for the sake of ostentation' — अव्ययीभाव-like तत्पुरुष ending in -अर्थम्) introduces the second rajasic motive. विद्धि (लोट् मध्यम एकवचन of √विद्) with double accusative तं यज्ञम् … राजसम् makes Krishna's verdict a direct imperative addressed to Arjuna (भरतश्रेष्ठ, सम्बोधन).

17.13.title: Five negations define tāmasa yajña
17.13.body: Five negatively-loaded adjectives stack up: विधिहीनम् ('without rule' — हीन with तृतीया sense), असृष्टान्नम् ('with no food distributed' — नञ् + बहुव्रीहि), मन्त्रहीनम् ('without mantras'), अदक्षिणम् ('without fee' — नञ्तत्पुरुष), श्रद्धाविरहितम् ('devoid of faith' — विरहित with तृतीया sense). All are नपुंसक द्वितीया agreeing with यज्ञम्.

17.13.title: परिचक्षते — they call it tāmasa
17.13.body: परिचक्षते (लट् आत्मनेपद बहुवचन of परि-√चक्ष् — 'they describe / they call') is the verse's verb; the impersonal 'they' is the tradition itself. Note that श्रद्धाविरहितम् is the last and weightiest of the five — for a chapter whose root question was about faith, faithlessness is the deepest disqualification.

17.14.title: शारीरं तपः — the bodily disciplines
17.14.body: Five practices in three pairs are listed as nominatives: देवद्विजगुरुप्राज्ञपूजनम् (a long तत्पुरुष — 'worship of gods, brāhmaṇas, gurus, the wise'), शौचम्, आर्जवम्, ब्रह्मचर्यम्, अहिंसा. The predicate शारीरं तपः ('bodily austerity,' नपुंसक प्रथमा) is identified with all five by the impersonal उच्यते (लट् कर्मणि — 'is called').

17.14.title: Body-tapas is largely about others
17.14.body: Four of the five items are relational — worship, purity, straightforwardness, non-injury — even ब्रह्मचर्य involves restraint vis-à-vis others. Krishna's शारीर तपः is not self-mortification (that was 17.5–6) but body-as-medium-of-conduct, the body in social and ritual space.

17.15.title: वाङ्मयं तपः — speech-austerity in four traits
17.15.body: वाक्यम् (नपुंसक प्रथमा) is qualified by four adjectives: अनुद्वेगकरम् ('non-agitating' — उपपद तत्पुरुष / कर्तृवाचक, with कृदन्त कर from √कृ taking अनुद्वेग as object), सत्यम्, प्रियम्, हितम्. To these is added स्वाध्यायाभ्यसनम् ('the practice of recitation of one's own veda' — षष्ठी-तत्पुरुष). The collective is named वाङ्मयं तपः, where -मय (मयट्) means 'consisting of speech'.

17.15.title: Priya AND hita — pleasant and beneficial together
17.15.body: Sanskrit ethics here refuses either pole alone: a sentence may be true but cruel, or pleasant but harmful. Krishna requires the conjunction — सत्यम् + प्रियम् + हितम् — as one compound discipline, with अनुद्वेगकरम् ensuring even the manner does not wound.

17.16.title: मानसं तपः — five interior practices
17.16.body: Five abstract nominatives form the list: मनःप्रसादः ('clarity of mind'), सौम्यत्वम् ('gentleness'), मौनम् ('silence'), आत्मविनिग्रहः ('self-restraint' — षष्ठी-तत्पुरुष), भावसंशुद्धिः ('purification of intention'). The capping demonstrative एतत् (नपुंसक प्रथमा) is identified by उच्यते with मानसं तपः.

17.16.title: Body, speech, mind — a graded interiority
17.16.body: 17.14–16 form a tightly designed triad — शारीरम् / वाङ्मयम् / मानसम् — each verse using the same closing template तपः … उच्यते. The triad will be summed up in 17.17 as 'this threefold austerity'; the architecture is deliberate and worth preserving in translation.

17.17.title: तत्त्रिविधं तपः — sattva integrates the three
17.17.body: तपः तत् त्रिविधम् (नपुंसक एकवचन) refers back to 17.14–16. The तृतीया plural नरैः युक्तैः अफलाकाङ्क्षिभिः ('by men yoked, free of fruit-craving') marks the agents, while श्रद्धया परया (तृतीया एकवचन of श्रद्धा with adjective परा — 'with supreme faith') marks the inner condition.

17.17.title: तप्तं तपः — heat heated rightly
17.17.body: तप्तम् is the कर्मणि भूतकृदन्त (past passive participle) of √तप्, agreeing with तपः — 'heated' / 'undergone.' Together with the cognate noun, the line literally says 'the heat heated by men with supreme faith.' सात्त्विकम् as predicate (नपुंसक एकवचन) is the verdict.

17.18.title: सत्कारमानपूजार्थम् — tapas for honour
17.18.body: The compound सत्कारमानपूजार्थम् ('for the sake of respect, honour, and worship' — द्वन्द्व ending in -अर्थम्) names the rajasic motive. क्रियते (लट् कर्मणि एकवचन of √कृ — 'is performed') is the impersonal verb; the agent is hidden, which itself fits the showy-but-empty quality.

17.18.title: चलम् अध्रुवम् — unstable, transient
17.18.body: Two predicate adjectives — चलम् ('shaky') and अध्रुवम् ('non-fixed' — नञ्तत्पुरुष) — describe the result, not the action. Even when the act itself is performed, its standing is provisional: applause is the only fruit, and applause is the very paradigm of चल and अध्रुव.

17.19.title: मूढग्राहेण — penance driven by deluded conviction
17.19.body: The तृतीया एकवचन मूढग्राहेण ('with deluded grasping' — कर्मधारय) names the inner driver. The instrumental आत्मनः पीडया ('with self-torment,' षष्ठी subordinate) shows the means: pain inflicted on oneself. The विकल्प परस्य उत्सादनार्थम् ('or for the destruction of another') adds the malevolent variant.

17.19.title: तत् तामसम् उदाहृतम् — declared tāmasa
17.19.body: उदाहृतम् is the कर्मणि भूतकृदन्त of उद्-आ-√हृ ('is cited / is declared'), neuter singular agreeing with तपः. Note that tāmasa tapas is the only category in which harming others appears as a motive — even rajasic display did not aim at उत्सादन.

17.20.title: दातव्यम् इति — giving as duty itself
17.20.body: The कृत्य/gerundive दातव्यम् ('it is to be given') quoted by इति gives the inner attitude. दीयते (लट् कर्मणि एकवचन of √दा — 'is given') is the verb; the recipient अनुपकारिणे (चतुर्थी एकवचन — 'to one who cannot return the favour,' नञ्तत्पुरुष) is the structural marker that the gift has no exchange-logic.

17.20.title: देशे काले पात्रे — place, time, fit recipient
17.20.body: Three सप्तमी singulars in series — देशे, काले, पात्रे — set the conditions: the right place, the right time, the worthy recipient. सात्त्विकं स्मृतम् ('is remembered/declared as sāttvika' — स्मृतम् is कर्मणि भूतकृदन्त of √स्मृ) closes the verse with traditional authority.

17.21.title: प्रत्युपकारार्थम् — giving for return
17.21.body: Two rajasic motives are listed: प्रत्युपकारार्थम् ('for the sake of a return favour' — तत्पुरुष ending in -अर्थम्) and फलम् उद्दिश्य ('aiming at a fruit' — उद्दिश्य is the absolutive of उद्-√दिश्). The combination ties the gift to expected output, in direct contrast to 17.20's अनुपकारिणे.

17.21.title: परिक्लिष्टम् — given begrudgingly
17.21.body: परिक्लिष्टम् ('grudgingly / with difficulty' — कर्मणि भूतकृदन्त of परि-√क्लिश्), नपुंसक प्रथमा agreeing with दानम्, captures the affective quality. Even where the gift happens, the inner reluctance damages its standing — rajasa is as much a phenomenology as an ethics.

17.22.title: अदेशकाले — gift at the wrong moment
17.22.body: अदेशकाले is a सप्तमी एकवचन of the dvandva compound (with नञ् prefix) — 'at a non-place-and-time.' अपात्रेभ्यः (चतुर्थी बहुवचन — 'to unfit recipients') completes the structural negation of 17.20's देशे काले पात्रे triad. The verb दीयते is again लट् कर्मणि.

17.22.title: असत्कृतम् अवज्ञातम् — disrespect baked in
17.22.body: Two past passive participles describe the giving itself: असत्कृतम् ('not honoured' — नञ् + कृत of सत्-√कृ) and अवज्ञातम् ('despised' — नञ् + कृत of अव-√ज्ञा). Tāmasa giving is not just mis-targeted — it carries contempt for the very act, which is its deepest defect.

17.23.title: ॐ तत् सत् — threefold designation of Brahman
17.23.body: The famous trio is announced as ब्रह्मणः निर्देशः त्रिविधः ('the threefold designation of Brahman,' षष्ठी + प्रथमा). स्मृतः (कर्मणि भूतकृदन्त of √स्मृ, पुल्लिंग प्रथमा agreeing with निर्देशः) anchors the formula in remembered tradition. इति demarcates the cited mantra.

17.23.title: ब्राह्मणाः वेदाः यज्ञाः — three derivations
17.23.body: तेन (तृतीया एकवचन — 'by that [threefold designation]') governs three प्रथमा बहुवचन subjects: ब्राह्मणाः, वेदाः, यज्ञाः, all qualified by विहिताः ('ordained,' कर्मणि भूतकृदन्त of वि-√धा). पुरा ('of old') gives the temporal frame — these are not Krishna's inventions but primordial uses of OṂ TAT SAT.

17.24.title: तस्मात् … ॐ इति उदाहृत्य — beginning with OṂ
17.24.body: तस्मात् (पञ्चमी एकवचन — 'therefore') draws the conclusion. उदाहृत्य is the absolutive (ल्यप् of उद्-आ-√हृ — 'having uttered'). The compound यज्ञदानतपःक्रियाः ('yajña-dāna-tapas-acts,' द्वन्द्व + तत्पुरुष, स्त्रीलिंग बहुवचन) names the three acts in one breath.

17.24.title: ब्रह्मवादिनाम् — perpetual practice of brahma-speakers
17.24.body: The षष्ठी बहुवचन ब्रह्मवादिनाम् ('of the speakers of Brahman') marks the subject-group; प्रवर्तन्ते (लट् आत्मनेपद बहुवचन of प्र-√वृत् — 'proceed / commence') is governed by them. सततम् ('always') and विधानोक्ताः ('as enjoined by the rule') keep the act simultaneously scriptural and habitual.

17.25.title: तत् — performed without aiming at fruit
17.25.body: तत् इति (the second of the three syllables) is paired with अनभिसन्धाय फलम् ('without aiming at fruit' — नञ् + absolutive of अभि-सम्-√धा, the negated counterpart of 17.12's अभिसन्धाय). The same verb-pair that earlier defined rajasic yajña now, negated, defines a higher mode.

17.25.title: मोक्षकाङ्क्षिभिः — for seekers of liberation
17.25.body: मोक्षकाङ्क्षिभिः (तृतीया बहुवचन — 'by those craving liberation') is the agent of क्रियन्ते (लट् कर्मणि बहुवचन — 'are performed'). The verbs apply to yajña, tapas, and the varied (विविधाः) acts of dāna alike: TAT marks the renunciation of fruit across all three.

17.26.title: सत् — in 'being' and 'goodness'
17.26.body: Two locatives — सद्भावे ('in the sense of existence') and साधुभावे ('in the sense of goodness') — give the two semantic registers in which सत् is used. प्रयुज्यते (लट् कर्मणि एकवचन of प्र-√युज् — 'is employed') is the verb; इति marks the quoted syllable.

17.26.title: प्रशस्ते कर्मणि — in a praised act
17.26.body: The सप्तमी प्रशस्ते कर्मणि ('in a praiseworthy act' — कर्मणि भूतकृदन्त of प्र-√शंस्) extends सत् beyond ontology and ethics to performance: a deed worthy of praise is itself called सत्. युज्यते (लट् कर्मणि — 'is fitting / is applied') echoes प्रयुज्यते and seals the semantic widening.

17.27.title: स्थितिः — steadfastness named सत्
17.27.body: The सप्तमी triad यज्ञे तपसि दाने ('in yajña, in tapas, in dāna') localises the locus; स्थितिः (स्त्रीलिंग प्रथमा — 'standing / firmness') is what is so named. उच्यते (लट् कर्मणि — 'is called') with the quoted सत् इति makes the naming explicit.

17.27.title: तदर्थीयं कर्म — work done for that's sake
17.27.body: कर्म तदर्थीयम् ('work done for the sake of that' — तदर्थीय is a possessive/purposive derivative of तदर्थम्) is also named सत्. अभिधीयते (लट् कर्मणि एकवचन of अभि-√धा — 'is designated') with एव ('itself') gives the line its emphatic closure: work in service of Brahman *is* sat.

17.28.title: अश्रद्धया — three offerings undone
17.28.body: अश्रद्धया (तृतीया एकवचन of अश्रद्धा — 'with non-faith,' नञ्तत्पुरुष) governs four past passive participles: हुतम् (√हु, offered as oblation), दत्तम् (√दा, given), तप्तम् (√तप्, undergone as tapas), and कृतम् (√कृ, done in general). All are नपुंसक एकवचन, generalising across rite-types.

17.28.title: असत् — neither here nor hereafter
17.28.body: उच्यते (लट् कर्मणि) with the quoted असत् इति inverts the previous five verses' सत्. The closing न च तत् प्रेत्य नो इह ('and that [bears fruit] neither after death nor here') uses प्रेत्य (absolutive of प्र-√इ — 'having departed') to deny both worlds at once. Krishna ends the chapter exactly where Arjuna's question (17.1) began — on the necessity of श्रद्धा.
